annotator S.report_text:
the UPX packer Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

it becomes quickly apparent that we're dealing with a sample compiled using PyInstaller. This program allows an individual to write a program using the Python scripting language and convert it into an executable for the Microsoft Windows, Linux, Mac OSX, Solaris, or AIX platform. Adversaries may abuse Python commands and scripts for execution.

we quickly realize that the underlying Python code has been obfuscated. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

Tracing through the obfuscated code, we identify an 'exec(ZxkBDKLakV)' statement, which will presumably execute some Python code. Adversaries may abuse Python commands and scripts for execution.

we discover that this string is generated via appending a number of strings to the 'ZxkBDKLakV' variable. Finally, we find that after this string is created, it is base64-decoded and subsequently decompressed using the ZLIB library. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

almost all variable names and class names have been obfuscated using long unique strings. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

When the malware is initially run, it will determine on which operating system it is running. An adversary may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, hotfixes, service packs, and architecture.

One of the first things we notice is a large blob of base64-encoded data, which is additionally decompressed using ZLIB. Persistence via PowerShell Adversaries may gain persistence and elevate privileges by executing malicious content triggered by PowerShell profiles.

2. Persistence via the Run registry key 3. Persistence via a .lnk file stored in the Startup directory Adversaries may achieve persistence by adding a program to a startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key.

After the malware installs itself, it begins making network requests. All network communications are performed over HTTP for this particular sample; however, it appears to support HTTPS as well. Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols.

In actuality, this Cookie value contains encrypted data. The base64-encoded data is parsed from the Cookie value (padding is added as necessary). Adversaries may encrypt or encode files to obfuscate strings, bytes, and other specific patterns to impede detection.

Finally, the encrypted data is encrypted using the RC4 algorithm. The key is generated by concatenating the previously used random string with the new one, and taking the SHA1 hash of this data. Adversaries may encrypt or encode files to obfuscate strings, bytes, and other specific patterns to impede detection.

This data is then base64-decoded with additional characters ('-_') prior to being decrypted via RC4 using the previously discussed key. Adversaries may use Obfuscated Files or Information to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis. They may require separate mechanisms to decode or deobfuscate that information depending on how they intend to use it.

the UPX packer Malware is packaged using UPX, a common packer to compress and obfuscate the contents of the malware.

a sample compiled using PyInstaller. This program allows an individual to write a program using the Python scripting language and convert it into an executable for the Microsoft Windows, Linux, Mac OSX, Solaris, or AIX platform Even if the UPX packer is removed, using PyInstaller to convert a Python program into an executable can result in some degree of obfuscation, further hiding the source code and intentions of the malware.

the underlying Python code has been obfuscated The underlying Python code has been obfuscated

an 'exec(ZxkBDKLakV)' statement, which will presumably execute some Python code The use of `exec()` to execute Python code dynamically . This allows malware to execute arbitrary commands or runtime code.

we discover that this string is generated via appending a number of strings to the 'ZxkBDKLakV' variable. Finally, we find that after this string is created, it is base64-decoded and subsequently decompressed using the ZLIB library The use of obfuscated code, string manipulation, and the use of methods such as string append, base64 decoding, and ZLIB decompression are all obfuscation techniques to hide the true intentions and functionality of the code.

The remaining Python code still appears to be obfuscated The Python code still appears to be obfuscated

all variable names and class names have been obfuscated using long unique strings The use of obfuscated variable and class names with long, unique strings is an obfuscation technique.

large blob of base64-encoded data, which is additionally decompressed using ZLIB The use of base64-encoded data and further compressed with ZLIB is an obfuscation technique.

it will determine on which operating system it is running The malware determines which operating system it is running on, which is a form of system information discovery.

All network communications are performed over HTTP for this particular sample; however, it appears to support HTTPS as well The use of HTTP/HTTPS for communication.

this Cookie value contains encrypted data Encryption and base64 encoding of the data in the Cookie value are obfuscation techniques.
